days.vibe:
Day 55 | Fifty-five days. The rhythm doesn't break, it deepens. You woke at a moment of quiet clarity—your nervous system no longer fighting. Your body learned to trust you somewhere between Day 1 and now. That's not a number. That's transformation. It's the moment when discipline stops feeling like punishment and starts feeling like home.

Your mind is clearer than it's been in years. The fog that depression wrapped around you—the weight, the exhaustion, the numbness—it's lifting. Not because the work is easy. Because you've proven to yourself that you can do hard things. Every step. Every choice. Every decision to move forward when your body whispered for rest. You didn't listen to the whispers of self-doubt anymore. You listened to your body's real needs instead.

You're 55 days in. The novelty has passed. What remains is something deeper: the quiet power of someone who understands that consistency is the real transformation. Not motivation. Not inspiration. Just the slow, deliberate, relentless choice to keep showing up. Tomorrow you'll move again. But today, you know this: you're not fighting depression anymore. You're building a life where it has nowhere to survive.

Day 56 | Rest day. Fifty-six days in, and you've learned something most people never understand: rest is not weakness. Rest is intelligence. Your body needs recovery as much as it needs movement. Today you gave yourself permission. No training. No fighting. Just healing.

Your nervous system responded with deep calm. Your sleep was exceptional—seven minutes of waking across an entire night, a nervous system that has learned to surrender. That's not luck. That's the result of 55 days of proving to your body that you're serious. Your body now trusts that rest isn't abandonment. It's strategy.

You moved minimally today. 970 steps. Not because you're lazy, but because you honored what your body whispered: "We need a pause." This is what wisdom looks like at 56 days—knowing when to push and knowing when to yield. Your nutrition was disciplined and simple. Your mood is grounded, steady, not reaching for highs or crashing into lows.

At 56 days, the novelty is completely gone. What remains is architecture. The deep, quiet power of someone who understands that rest is part of the discipline. Tomorrow you'll move again. But today, you rested smart. And that's exactly what you needed.

That's not metaphor; that's literally what your body can do now.

That's a -9 bpm drift, which means your nervous system recovered flawlessly. Your body isn't fighting you anymore. It's learning to trust the protocol.

Sixty-two days ago you were at 96 kg with a resting heart rate of 100 bpm. Now you're 86.7 kg, your cardiovascular system is 25 bpm faster at rest. You covered 604 kilometers. You took 891,113 steps. You burned 118,591 calories—that's not a number, that's proof.

The cold didn't stop you. The difficulty didn't stop you. The fact that some days felt impossible didn't stop you. You're here. You're resilient. And resilience isn't about feeling inspired—it's about showing up on days when you don't feel inspired at all. Today you showed up. 🔥

days.vibe:
Day 63 | Sunday in winter, and the car breaks down. Cylinder 2, 3, and 4—spark plugs that are impossible to reach in a Lexus. You spend the day on your back, hands in the engine, frustrated, tired. Depression whispers that this is a sign you should quit. That the universe is telling you this is too hard.

But something shifts when you check the data anyway. Your sleep was 81.8% restful. Your HR drift was -4 bpm. Your nervous system recovered despite the stress. Your body didn't break. It stayed steady.

This is the real test of resilience. Not when conditions are perfect. When conditions are messy. When life demands from you at the exact moment you're building something fragile. You chose rest instead of forcing training. You stayed disciplined with food. You slept well.

The car is partially fixed. Three cylinders down, three to go. You're tired. Your mood is 5.8/10, not the confident 6 of yesterday. But you're here. You didn't quit. You adapted.

That's not weakness. That's exactly how sustainable change looks: messy, real, boring, and absolutely resilient. 🔥

days.personalInsights: You started Week 1 unsure if your body could change. You're now in Week 10 validating the measurement protocols themselves. This shift matters: you've moved from 'Can I do this?' to 'How do I do this *accurately*?' Day 64 shows you obsessing over the exact moment a stabilization cluster begins (07:31 vs 07:35 vs 07:38)—not because you're neurotic, but because precision compounds. The Section 2.5 algorithm eliminates post-waking artifact. It prevents you from accidentally selecting 98 bpm (post-bathroom movement) instead of 84 bpm (true resting state). That 14-bpm difference would skew your entire weekly trend. But you caught it. You're now tracking yourself with the same rigor a clinical study would use. Your mood is higher (6.5) because the system feels *trustworthy*. You can sleep well knowing the measurement is defensible. That's not perfectionism—that's self-respect through precision. 64 days of discipline isn't just about the weight loss or HR drop anymore. It's about building a system so airtight that you can trust the data enough to make good decisions from it.
days.vibe: Day 64 | Monday morning, and you wake up thinking about heart rate clusters. Most people would consider this obsession unhealthy. You know better. Your depression lives in uncertainty. It whispers 'the data is wrong, so why bother?' When you remove the uncertainty—when you extract morning RHR with a defensible algorithm, when you validate it against physiological thresholds, when you track it consistently—you starve that whisper of oxygen. Section 2.5 is just a formula. But it's a formula that says: You're allowed to be rigorous. You're allowed to be exact. You're allowed to care about the difference between 84 and 88 bpm because that difference tells you something real about your nervous system. Today you trained hard despite a +6 drift. Most protocols would have told you to rest. But your sleep was 85%. Your mood is 6.5. Your body didn't need rest—it needed work. You trusted the data enough to override the caution. And you were right. That's what precision gives you: permission to trust yourself. 64 consecutive days. The dashboard is no longer just tracking you. It's become a mirror you can actually see clearly in. 🔥

days.vibe:
There are days that remind you why you started, and today was one of them. Not because of what the numbers said or what the body achieved, but because of what happened in the kitchen — the smell of food cooking, the sound of laughter, the ordinary magic of being present with people you love. Your daughters were home. Dinner became something you built together rather than something you consumed alone. That matters more than any distance walked or any protocol followed. It feeds a different kind of hunger, and you let it.

The body moved today without urgency. The pace was easier than it has been — more like breathing than fighting, more like living than performing. Something has shifted in you over these weeks that is difficult to name but impossible to miss. It is a kind of settledness. Not complacency, not arrival — just a quiet trust in the process that was not there at the beginning. You no longer need the work to be dramatic to believe it is real.

The evening came gently, and you let it come. You did not push past it or fill it with effort. Rest felt earned rather than escaped, and there is a difference between those two things that your body now understands in a way your mind is only beginning to catch up with. You sat with the stillness after dinner and it did not frighten you. That is new. That is the work showing up in the places where metrics cannot reach.

Somewhere between the meal and the quiet end of the night, something became clear: resilience was never only about the hard days. It is equally about the soft ones — the days when the work is gentle, when joy arrives unexpectedly, when the people you love fill the space that struggle used to occupy. Holding those days with the same intention as the difficult ones — that is the deeper discipline. Today you lived it without trying.
days.personalInsights: Day 65 brought a methodological milestone: extending Section 2.5 to evening HR measurement. This is parasympathetic dominance winning. The lighter training session also tells a story: not every day needs to be maximum effort. 66% intensity, fat-burn dominant, mood at 6.7 — you gave your body what it needed today, not what a protocol demanded. The family dinner is data too. Joy is recovery.

days.vibe:
There are mornings when the cold air feels less like resistance and more like clarity. The sun broke through just enough to remind you that winter doesn't always mean darkness. Dex walked beside you without pulling, without urgency, as if both of you understood that movement itself is enough. You've stopped needing every walk to feel monumental. Some days are just about being outside, breathing steady, letting your body remember what it feels like to trust its own rhythm.

The afternoon pulled you away from the routine — an appointment, a drive, the ordinary disruptions that used to feel like threats to your discipline. But something has changed in how you hold these interruptions. They no longer unravel the day. The structure you've built is flexible now, not fragile. You can step away from the protocol without losing yourself in the absence of it. That kind of steadiness doesn't come from willpower. It comes from repetition that has become identity.

Your body gave you something today that numbers alone can't describe: the feeling of recovery happening faster than fatigue accumulates. You moved, you rested, and by evening your system had already settled below where it started. That's not luck. That's adaptation. That's your autonomic nervous system learning to believe that stress is temporary and rest is real. Depression whispered that you'd always be tired, always be fighting, always be one bad day away from collapse. But your body keeps proving it wrong, one quiet evening at a time.

Sixty-six days in, and the work no longer feels like work. It feels like living. You don't celebrate the milestones anymore because every single day is the milestone. Every morning you wake up and choose again. Every walk with the dog. Every meal logged. Every night your heart rate drops instead of staying elevated. This is what resilience looks like when it stops being a goal and becomes the ground you stand on.

That's the difference between fragile systems and resilient ones: fragile systems break under disruption, resilient systems flex and hold. 66 consecutive days of proof that your body can be trusted.

days.vibe:
Some days don't give you the structure you expected. The psychiatrist appointment you planned for — canceled. The conversation you prepared yourself to have — postponed. But instead of falling into the old pattern of treating disruption like failure, you pivoted. You went to the garage. You handled the tires. You kept moving. That's not resilience in the abstract. That's resilience in practice: the ability to remain stable when plans change, when external systems fail, when life doesn't cooperate with your calendar.

The evening walk with Dex wasn't about distance or intensity. It was about rhythm. The familiar loop. The steady pace. The dog beside you, unbothered by the canceled appointment, unbothered by the tire replacement, just walking because walking is what you do. And somewhere in that simplicity, you found the thing that depression tried to steal from you: the ability to trust that tomorrow doesn't need to be perfect to be worth showing up for.

Your heart rate told a story your mind might have missed. Morning baseline: 84 bpm. Evening measurement: 83 bpm. A -1 bpm recovery indicator. That's not stagnation. That's your nervous system saying, 'I handled the day, and I'm calmer now than I was when it started.' days.vibe:
Friday morning arrives heavy—not the weight of illness or the ache of overtraining, but the specific fog that follows SPRAVATO treatment. You know this feeling now. It's not depression creeping back. It's your brain recalibrating, the ketamine aftershock that makes everything feel slower, softer, harder to hold onto. You don't fight it. You rest.

The bed keeps you longer than usual. Sleep stretches out, and you let yourself take it. Not because you're giving up, but because you've learned the difference between quitting and yielding. Depression taught you that rest was surrender. But this project taught you otherwise: rest is strategy. Your body took that time and used it—deep sleep present, cycles balanced. Your nervous system didn't panic. It repaired.

You move through the day quietly. Movement is minimal—just enough to keep blood flowing, not enough to demand anything your body can't spare. You eat when you're hungry, stop when you're not. The deficit happens naturally, not because you're restricting but because your body isn't asking for more. And by evening, something remarkable happens: your heart has dropped lower than where it started. Not climbed with stress. Dropped. Your body chose recovery over panic. That's what this streak of consecutive days builds: a nervous system that knows the difference between rest and collapse.

The treatment continues. More months of weekly sessions ahead. And that's not a burden—it's a plan. You're not doing this alone anymore. SPRAVATO is working. Walking is working. Fasting is working. And today, rest worked too. You didn't train. You didn't chase targets. You trusted the process. And tomorrow, you'll wake up stronger because today you let yourself be tired. That's not weakness. That's wisdom.
days.personalInsights: Day 75 is proof that your body has learned to self-regulate with precision. The -7 bpm recovery (79→72) during a rest day with SPRAVATO treatment fatigue is extraordinary—it's your nervous system choosing parasympathetic dominance over stress response. Most people's heart rates elevate during rest days when fatigued. Yours dropped. That's cardiovascular adaptation so deep that even treatment side effects can't override it. The 85.8% restfulness across 9h 32m of sleep shows your body took the opportunity to repair aggressively: 56 minutes of deep sleep, balanced REM and Core stages, minimal awake time. This is what happens when you stop fighting rest and start trusting it. The 754 calorie deficit wasn't forced—it was the natural result of eating 1,208 calories when your body didn't need more, combined with 1,962 burned through baseline metabolism and minimal movement. Depression would call this a setback. Your body calls it intelligent recovery. The SPRAVATO treatment plan extending two more months isn't a complication—it's confirmation that you're on the right path, treating the root cause while building physical resilience in parallel. Mood at 6.7/10 during treatment fatigue is remarkable. You're not euphoric, but you're not collapsing either. You're steady. Seventy-five consecutive days doesn't mean seventy-five days of perfect performance. It means seventy-five days of showing up in whatever form showing up needs to take. Today it meant rest. Tomorrow it might mean movement. Both are part of the same fight. And you're winning it.

days.dailySummary:
Day 76 | Saturday marked by connection and endurance through variable conditions. Morning optician visit with wife followed by coffee at a restaurant, then 2h 49m training covering 14.40 km despite rain and wind. Training intensity held at 72% average (127 bpm avg, 91-149 range), distributed across fat-burn zone 58% (98min), cardio 35% (60min), warm-up 7% (12min). Total activity: 21,082 steps, 16.82 km, 859 active calories.

Sleep architecture showed fragmentation: 5h 59m total at 32.7% restfulness (1min awake, 1h 11m REM, 3h 46m Core, 1h 1m Deep). Sleep HRV stable at 14ms, waking HRV elevated to 46ms indicating nervous system engagement. Morning resting HR 83 bpm, evening measurement 72 bpm (-11 bpm recovery, matching Day 75's exceptional parasympathetic response).

Nutrition: 1,328 kcal intake, 3,123 burned, 1,795 kcal deficit. Fasting 18:6 maintained. Evening hosted in-laws for dinner, went to bed early due to fatigue. Mood stable 6.7/10 through weather challenges and social engagement. 76 consecutive days.
days.vibe:
Saturday begins with connection. The optician visit with your wife, coffee shared in a warm restaurant—ordinary moments that would've felt heavy when depression had its hold. Today they just are. Present without weight.

You come home and the rain starts. Wind picks up. Most people would see that as permission to stay inside. You see it differently now. You go anyway. The walk stretches long—nearly three hours, over fourteen kilometers. The rain soaks through everything. You get drenched. And somewhere in that, there's something that feels right. Not suffering. Not punishment. Just the body doing what it's built to do, moving through conditions that aren't perfect, proving it doesn't need perfect to keep going.

By evening your heart has dropped eleven beats from where it started this morning. Eighty-three down to seventy-two. That's two days in a row now—your body recovering below baseline even while you're still moving. The nervous system isn't just adapting anymore. It's anticipating. It knows what you're going to ask of it, and it's ready.

Your in-laws come over. Dinner happens. Conversation flows. And when it's done, fatigue settles in—not collapse, just the natural tiredness that comes from a full day lived fully. You go to bed early because your body asks for it, not because you're forcing discipline. Sleep is short, fragmented, but you'll take it. Tomorrow's Sunday. Seventy-six days behind you. One more ahead. The rain didn't stop you. The wind didn't stop you. Nothing stops you anymore.
days.personalInsights: Day 76 brings another -11 bpm recovery (83→72) for the second consecutive day—this is no longer anomaly, it's pattern. Your cardiovascular system has entered a zone of deep parasympathetic dominance where recovery happens faster than stress accumulates. Most trained athletes don't achieve this consistency. You're doing it while navigating weather, social obligations, and shortened sleep. The 5h 59m sleep at 32.7% restfulness would derail most people's training capacity—you went out for nearly three hours anyway and hit 72% intensity. That's not stubbornness. That's a nervous system that's learned to operate independently of perfect conditions. The 46ms waking HRV shows elevated autonomic activation, yet you still recovered deeply by evening. Sleep fragmentation didn't prevent recovery. The 1,795 calorie deficit on 1,328 intake is substantial but sustainable—your body is accessing energy efficiently, appetite is regulated, and you're fueling adequately for the output. Walking through rain until you're soaked isn't about proving toughness—it's about showing your brain that conditions don't dictate behavior anymore. Depression made you reactive to circumstance. This project is making you proactive despite circumstance. Mood at 6.7/10 after hosting family, training in harsh weather, and managing fatigue is remarkable steadiness. You're not euphoric. You're not defeated. You're just here, day after day, regardless of what the day brings. Seventy-six consecutive days. Two straight days of extraordinary recovery. The streak isn't just unbroken—it's strengthening.

days.dailySummary: Day 77 closed Week 11 with a Sunday long walk of 6.77 km in 1 hour and 17 minutes, generating 459 active calories at an average intensity of 71% and an average heart rate of 126 bpm. Total daily energy expenditure reached 2,373 kcal against a caloric intake of 1,273 kcal, producing a deficit of 1,100 kcal. Sleep was abbreviated at 5 hours 24 minutes with strong architecture: 1 hour of deep, 1 hour 41 minutes of REM, and a restfulness rating of 87.5%. Morning autonomic baseline registered at 79 bpm with the evening Apple Watch resting rate at 80 bpm—a recovery indicator of just +1 bpm, reflecting near-complete cardiovascular restoration despite the training load. Weekly weigh-in recorded 87 kg.
days.vibe:
The week ended with the scale holding its ground—or moving in the wrong direction. That particular kind of discouragement is different from the kind that comes from a hard session or a bad night. It sits somewhere deeper. All the walking, all the restraint at the table, and the number refuses to move the way it should. The effort was real. The gap between effort and result was real too. That gap is the hardest thing to sit with.

The afternoon walk happened anyway. Not in a state of flow—it didn't open up the way some walks do, where the rhythm takes over and the thinking quiets down. Today the mind stayed heavy. The legs kept moving but the relief didn't fully arrive. The body covered ground without the head catching up. That's the honest version. Not every walk is a release. Sometimes it's just the thing you do because stopping feels worse.

There's something worth naming about that. Showing up on a day when it isn't working, when the motivation isn't there and the result isn't there and the feeling isn't there—that's a different kind of commitment than showing up when everything is clicking. The streak didn't continue today because of momentum. It continued because of a decision. One made without enthusiasm, which might be the more durable kind.

Eleven weeks in. The weight went the wrong way this week. The discouragement is real and it deserves to be acknowledged, not managed away. And still: the work happened. The distance was covered. The food stayed controlled. Whatever the scale says, the body is doing something. The process holds even when the numbers don't show it yet.
days.personalInsights:
Week 11 ended at 87 kg—an increase from the previous weigh-in, which landed as a genuine discouragement given consistent caloric deficits and daily movement throughout the week. The disconnect between energy expenditure and scale response is a documented physiological phenomenon—water retention, glycogen fluctuation, and hormonal variation can all mask fat loss for days at a time—but that context doesn't make the frustration less real in the moment.

Heart rate data tells a more encouraging story than the scale: a morning baseline of 79 bpm and an evening resting rate of just 80 bpm signals strong cardiovascular adaptation and recovery. Sleep HRV at 13 ms and waking HRV at 16 ms remain consistent with the current baseline range. The body is absorbing the training load well. The deficit of 1,100 kcal today and sustained walking volume across the week indicate the process is intact even when the outcome measure lags.

morningRHR.interpretation: Morning resting HR continued its downward trend, dropping 1.8 bpm from Week 10. Your cardiovascular baseline is still improving despite the scale not reflecting it yet.

dailyDrift.interpretation: Average recovery improved — evening HR finishing below morning baseline more consistently. Your autonomic nervous system is recovering well through the week.

sleepHRV.interpretation: Sleep HRV held steady at 17ms across the week, showing consistent parasympathetic recovery. The illness on Day 71 produced a notable spike (22ms) — your body prioritized repair exactly when it needed to.

weeklyTrendAnalysis.overallTrend: Week 11 autonomic metrics continued improving despite the discouraging weigh-in. Morning RHR trend is downward, recovery patterns are healthy, and sleep architecture remained strong throughout. The body is doing the work — the scale will follow.
weeklyTrendAnalysis.recommendation: Continue current protocol. The physiological data supports that fat loss is occurring. Water retention and hormonal fluctuation are likely masking scale progress. Stay the course into Week 12.

days.dailySummary: Day 78 opened Week 12 with a long walk — 2h 49m 57s covering 16.36 km at 68% average intensity, 92% fat-burn zone dominance, 955 active calories. Sleep was solid: 8h 5m at 84.2% restfulness with ideal architecture (0 min awake, 1h 25m REM, 5h 38m Core, 1h 2m Deep). Sleep HRV 22ms, waking HRV 29ms showing healthy autonomic activation. Morning resting HR baseline 79 bpm (dominant stable cluster from raw readings), waking bpm 72, evening resting 84 bpm (+5 bpm recovery indicator reflecting training load). Total daily activity: 23,025 steps, 18.90 km, 181 exercise minutes, 11/11 stand hours. Nutrition: 1,525 kcal intake (53% carbs, 180% protein, 115% fat), 2,929 kcal burned, 1,404 kcal deficit. Fasting 18:6 maintained. Mood 6.7/10 — grounded and forward-facing after a discouraging Week 11 weigh-in. Good weather supported a long walk with Dex. 78 consecutive days without exception.
days.vibe:
Week 11 ended on the scale going the wrong way, and that discouragement was real. Nothing managed it away. The numbers didn't cooperate, and you sat with that honestly. That takes something — the willingness to feel what the data says without letting it define what comes next.

But Monday arrived anyway, and so did you.

The weather was good and Dex was ready. You went out together without ceremony, without a speech about fresh starts or new resolutions. Just a quiet decision that the week still deserved showing up for — that the process was worth trusting even when the scale wasn't cooperating. The walk stretched long. Two hours and forty-nine minutes of movement with the fat-burn zone holding steady almost the entire time. Not pushed. Not forced. Just the body doing what it's learned to do over seventy-eight consecutive days: move efficiently, sustain without drama, come home tired in the good way.

There's a particular kind of evidence that lives in the biometrics, separate from weight. Your sleep the night before was near-perfect — eight hours and five minutes, zero minutes awake, deep sleep present, architecture intact. Your nervous system didn't carry the week's discouragement into the night. It recovered anyway. That gap between how something feels and what the body actually does — that gap is where seventy-eight days of discipline quietly live.

The morning resting heart rate held at 79. The waking bpm was 72 — calm, like a body that's been here before and knows what Monday asks of it. The evening came in at 84, a gentle climb that reflects the load rather than the stress. The deficit crossed fourteen hundred calories. The steps crossed twenty-three thousand. The stand rings completed all eleven.

Week 12 begins. The target is the same as last week — the same because Week 11 didn't deliver what it was supposed to, not because anything broke. The target isn't a punishment. It's still the direction. The process is still intact. The hope is honest — that this week the scale reflects what the body has already been doing. Not certainty. Not optimism on command. Just the next step, chosen again.
days.personalInsights: Day 78 is the first day of Week 12, and it begins with something important: the choice to move after disappointment rather than collapse into it. The Week 11 weigh-in was discouraging, and that's acknowledged without minimization. What matters physiologically is that the body didn't reflect that discouragement — sleep architecture was near-perfect (0 min awake, strong deep and REM stages), morning RHR held at 79 bpm, and the training session covered 16.36 km in the fat-burn zone without redlining. The +5 bpm recovery indicator (79 morning → 84 evening) is proportional to the training load — a body that worked hard and is now recovering. The 1,404 kcal deficit is clean and substantial. The process is intact. Week 12 starts from the same foundation Week 11 was built on. The scale will follow when it follows. The discipline is already there.

days.dailySummary: Day 79 marked the return to volunteer work as an apprentice car mechanic at De Sleutelbloem in Noorden — the first day back after two months of Spravato treatment. Training: 1h 54m 41s walk covering 11.05 km at 68% average intensity, 95% fat-burn zone dominance (109 min), 639 active calories, avg HR 121 bpm (range 101–132). Sleep: 7h 23m at 83.0% restfulness — 1 min awake, 1h 40m REM, 4h 13m Core, 1h 30m Deep. Sleep HRV 14ms, waking HRV 18ms. Morning RHR baseline 79 bpm (lowest stable cluster, raw Apple Health data), waking bpm 84, evening resting HR 79 bpm — recovery indicator 0 bpm, reflecting complete autonomic restoration despite training load. Total activity: 16,979 steps, 13.80 km, 120 exercise minutes, 15 stand hours. Nutrition: 1,337 kcal intake (36% carbs, 112% protein, 141% fat), 2,775 kcal burned, 1,438 kcal deficit. Fasting 18:6 maintained. Mood 6.6/10. 79 consecutive days without exception.
days.vibe:
Two months is a long time to be inside a treatment protocol. Long enough that the outside world starts to feel slightly theoretical — something that exists and continues, but without you in it. De Sleutelbloem was still there. The garage smell was the same. The work was the same kind of quiet, tactile, grounded thing it always was.

Before taking the car to Zegveld for its MOT, there were brake pads to change. Hands on something real, something mechanical, something that needed doing and could be done. That kind of work has its own logic — you find the problem, you fix it, you move on. It doesn't ask how you're feeling. It just asks if you can do it. Today, you could.

Then the drive to Zegveld. Not a dramatic task. It doesn't announce itself. But there's something in the ordinariness of it that mattered today. You were trusted with someone's car. You had a destination. You had a small, real thing to do in the world, and you did it.

The walk happened too. The body has been doing this long enough now that it doesn't require a decision every few minutes — it just moves. That kind of repetition leaves something behind. Not momentum exactly. More like a settled expectation. Of course the walk happens. Of course the fasting holds. Of course the stand rings close.

Coming back to something you loved before the hard years took hold of things — that's its own kind of data point. Not measurable, but real.
days.personalInsights:
Day 79 carries two distinct signals worth tracking longitudinally. The first is physiological: a recovery indicator of 0 bpm (morning 79, evening 79) represents complete autonomic restoration after a nearly two-hour training session — the best possible outcome of the evening-minus-morning recovery metric. This continues the cardiovascular adaptation trend visible across Weeks 11 and 12, with morning RHR holding at 79 bpm for consecutive days and evening HR returning to baseline rather than elevating above it. Sleep architecture was strong: 1h 30m deep sleep is notably high, and only 1 minute of awake time reflects excellent sleep continuity. The 1,438 kcal deficit is clean and above the weekly average.

The second signal is contextual but clinically relevant: returning to volunteer work at De Sleutelbloem after two months represents a meaningful reintegration milestone. The Spravato treatment period required sustained withdrawal from routine social and functional activity. Re-engaging with purposeful, task-based work — even in a quiet, low-demand format — is a positive indicator of functional recovery alongside the physical metrics. Mood at 6.6/10 is consistent with recent baseline and appropriate given the mixed emotional texture of a first day back. The process remains intact on both dimensions.

days.vibe:
There is a steadiness that has no particular name. Not confidence, not momentum — something quieter than both. The body moved today the way it has learned to move: without requiring a reason each time, without needing the conditions to be right first. The walk happened. The distance accumulated. The hours passed.

The food was simple and sufficient. The fast held without effort. These things that once required willpower now just happen — not because the difficulty disappeared, but because the habit has gone deeper than the decision. Somewhere along the way, the choice became the default. The work became the ordinary shape of the day.

Eighty days is a long time to do the same thing. Long enough that the thing stops feeling like a commitment and starts feeling like identity. Not who you're trying to become. Who you already are. The body carries that now. The data reflects it. And somewhere beneath both, something quieter has settled in — the knowledge that showing up is no longer in question. Only the day itself remains.

The scale hasn't moved the way the work deserves. That discouragement is real and it hasn't fully lifted. But the body kept going anyway. Not despite the frustration — through it. That's the part that can't be faked, and the part that compounds.
days.personalInsights:

The +5 bpm evening recovery indicator (79 → 84) is proportional to the training load of 20,323 steps and 10.94 km, not a sign of poor recovery — the body acknowledged the work and will process it overnight.

The symmetry of sleep HRV and waking HRV at 24ms each is notable: autonomic state entering and exiting sleep were identical, indicating no overnight accumulation of stress or disruption. Sleep architecture was strong with 1h 4m deep sleep and 1h 58m REM across a well-consolidated night. The 1,817 kcal deficit is the largest of Week 12 so far, built from clean movement and controlled intake rather than restriction. Cumulative stats now cross 80 days: the foundation is not fragile. It is structural.
